quiz_schema.question: string

options.A: string
options.B: string
options.C: string
options.D: string

quiz_schema.misconception_correction: boolean (true if this question targets a previous misconception)

topic_stat_schema.interval_days: number (days until next review, 0 = never asked)
topic_stat_schema.streak: number (consecutive correct answers, resets on wrong)
topic_stat_schema.misconception: boolean (true if last wrong answer had confidence >= 2)

quizzes.question: Which of the following is NOT a commonly used feature detector in computer vision?

options.A: SIFT (Scale-Invariant Feature Transform)
options.B: SURF (Speeded Up Robust Features)
options.C: ORB (Oriented FAST and Rotated BRIEF)
options.D: DIFT (Distributed Image Feature Transformation)

quizzes.question: In minimax with alpha-beta pruning, what is the maximum number of leaf nodes that must be evaluated in the best case?

options.A: The same as without pruning — all leaf nodes
options.B: Approximately O(b^(d/2))
options.C: Logarithmic in the tree size
options.D: O(b^d) but with reduced branching factor

quizzes.question: In Q-learning, which of the following is the main advantage over SARSA?

options.A: Q-learning is guaranteed to converge faster
options.B: Q-learning is off-policy and learns the optimal policy regardless of exploration strategy
options.C: Q-learning requires fewer episodes to converge
options.D: Q-learning uses a different discount factor

quizzes.question: What is the time complexity of the CYK (Cocke-Younger-Kasami) parsing algorithm for a sentence of length n?

options.A: O(n)
options.B: O(n²)
options.C: O(n³)
options.D: O(n⁴)

quizzes.question: In software engineering, what is the primary purpose of establishing a clear software requirements specification (SRS)?

options.A: To define the aesthetic design of the user interface
options.B: To create a formal agreement between stakeholders about what the software must do
options.C: To establish the coding standards the team will follow
options.D: To determine the project's marketing strategy

quizzes.question: In supervised learning, what is the primary characteristic that distinguishes it from other learning paradigms?

options.A: The model learns patterns from unlabeled data without any target variable
options.B: The model learns from labeled data where both input features and corresponding target/output values are provided during training
options.C: The model learns through interaction with an environment, receiving rewards or penalties for actions
options.D: The model learns by identifying groups or clusters in data without predefined labels

quizzes.question: In first-order logic, what is the primary difference between classical resolution and forward chaining for knowledge base inference?

options.A: Classical resolution is sound, while forward chaining is not
options.B: Forward chaining is goal-driven and searches backward from the query, while resolution uses a refutation approach
options.C: Forward chaining is data-driven and searches forward from facts, while resolution works backward from the negated goal
options.D: They are identical methods with different naming conventions

quizzes.question: In Bayesian inference, Bayes' theorem is expressed as P(A|B) = P(B|A) × P(A) / P(B). What does P(B|A) represent?

options.A: The probability of A given B has occurred
options.B: The probability of B given A has occurred
options.C: The prior probability of event A
options.D: The normalization constant

quizzes.question: What is the primary purpose of the Viterbi algorithm in Hidden Markov Models?

options.A: To calculate the probability of an observation sequence given a model
options.B: To find the most likely sequence of hidden states given an observation sequence
options.C: To estimate the parameters of an HMM from training data
options.D: To sample new observation sequences from an HMM

quizzes.question: You're building a decision tree classifier for fraud detection on a dataset with 10 million transactions, where only 1% are fraudulent (severe class imbalance). Features include a mix of continuous and categorical variables, and fraud patterns shift seasonally. Which configuration best addresses ALL of these constraints simultaneously?

options.A: ID3 with information gain, since its top-down greedy construction scales well to large datasets and it handles both continuous and categorical features natively.
options.B: CART (Gini impurity) with cost-sensitive splitting (higher misclassification cost for the fraud class), max_depth=10 to prevent overfitting, and scheduled periodic retraining to handle seasonal distribution shift.
options.C: C4.5 with gain ratio normalization, since gain ratio inherently corrects for class imbalance, eliminating the need for cost-sensitive learning.
options.D: A fully-grown CART tree (no depth limit) with SMOTE oversampling applied to the minority class, since balancing the training set alone resolves both the imbalance and overfitting issues.

quizzes.question: You are training a 4-layer neural network to predict hospital readmission risk (binary classification) from 50 patient features. After 100 epochs, training accuracy is 73% and validation accuracy is 72% — no overfitting — but the loss curve is nearly flat and gradients in the first two layers are consistently near zero. The network uses sigmoid activations throughout all hidden layers. Which change MOST directly targets the root cause of this convergence failure?

options.A: Add L2 regularization to all weight matrices, since large weights are amplifying the gradient signal and causing it to saturate before reaching early layers
options.B: Replace sigmoid activations with ReLU in the hidden layers, since sigmoid's output is bounded [0,1] and its derivative approaches zero in saturated regions, causing gradients to vanish as they propagate backward through multiple layers
options.C: Increase batch size from 32 to 512, since small batches introduce gradient noise that prevents the optimizer from finding consistent descent directions in deep networks
options.D: Add a softmax normalization layer before the output sigmoid, since the missing normalization step is the direct cause of the near-zero gradients in the early layers

quizzes.question: You have a dataset with 500 features and 1,000 samples. You apply PCA and find that the first 10 principal components explain 95% of the total variance. Which of the following statements best explains WHY PCA achieves this compression?

options.A: PCA selects the 10 original features that have the highest variance, discarding all others
options.B: PCA finds orthogonal directions (eigenvectors of the covariance matrix) along which the data varies most, allowing most variance to be captured in far fewer dimensions than the original feature space
options.C: PCA removes correlated features by randomly sampling 10 features and testing whether they reconstruct the original data within a 5% error threshold
options.D: PCA works by clustering the 500 features into 10 groups and replacing each group with its centroid

quizzes.question: You are building a panorama stitching pipeline that must align overlapping images taken at different scales and orientations. SIFT (Scale-Invariant Feature Transform) is proposed as the feature detector/descriptor. Which of the following correctly describes WHY SIFT is well-suited for this task?

options.A: SIFT detects features by applying a fixed-size Sobel edge filter at a single scale, making it fast but only effective when images have identical zoom levels
options.B: SIFT builds a scale-space by convolving the image with Gaussians at multiple scales, detects extrema in Difference-of-Gaussian (DoG) images across scales, and assigns dominant gradient orientations to each keypoint — achieving invariance to both scale and rotation changes
options.C: SIFT achieves scale invariance by resizing all input images to a canonical 256×256 resolution before extracting Harris corner features, which normalizes scale differences
options.D: SIFT uses a learned deep neural network to extract features that are inherently invariant to affine transformations, which is why it was introduced as the first learning-based detector

quizzes.question: A model trained on your dataset achieves 99% training accuracy but only 61% validation accuracy. A colleague suggests increasing model complexity to fix this. Why is that advice wrong, and what should you do instead?

options.A: The advice is wrong because the model is underfitting; you should decrease complexity to reduce variance
options.B: The advice is wrong because the model is overfitting (high variance); you should reduce complexity, add regularization, or get more training data
options.C: The advice is correct — higher complexity always reduces the gap between training and validation accuracy
options.D: The advice is wrong because the model has high bias; you should increase the learning rate to escape the local minimum

quizzes.question: You are designing an image matching system for an autonomous drone that must recognize landmarks from varying altitudes and camera angles. You need a feature detection and description pipeline robust to scale and rotation changes. Which pipeline would provide the MOST robust matching performance for this scenario?

options.A: Apply a Canny edge detector followed by template matching, since edge maps are inherently scale-invariant and capture structural information
options.B: Use Harris corner detection with fixed-scale windows, then match corners using normalized cross-correlation of surrounding patches
options.C: Use SIFT, which detects keypoints across a Difference-of-Gaussian scale space and generates 128-dimensional gradient histogram descriptors, providing robustness to both scale and rotation changes
options.D: Use simple pixel intensity comparisons (CENSUS transform) at a single resolution, since raw intensity features generalize better across viewpoints than gradient-based methods

quizzes.question: You are building a real-time visual odometry system for a mobile robot. The robot's camera captures frames at 30 fps, and you need to track features across consecutive frames to estimate motion. You test three detectors — Harris corners, SIFT, and ORB — and observe the following: Harris finds 1,200 corners per frame in 2ms but 40% of matches are incorrect after a 15° camera rotation. SIFT finds 800 keypoints in 120ms with 95% correct matches after rotation. ORB finds 900 keypoints in 5ms with 88% correct matches after rotation. Given the 33ms frame budget and the requirement for reliable matching under camera rotation, which detector is the BEST choice and WHY?

options.A: Harris corners — highest keypoint count ensures the most feature correspondences, and the 2ms runtime leaves ample budget for other processing stages
options.B: SIFT — 95% match accuracy is the only acceptable rate for visual odometry, and the 120ms runtime can be offset by skipping every other frame
options.C: ORB — it uses an oriented FAST detector with rotation-aware BRIEF descriptors, achieving strong rotation invariance (88% match rate) within the real-time budget (5ms << 33ms), unlike Harris which lacks rotation invariance and SIFT which exceeds the frame budget
options.D: Run SIFT on a GPU to bring runtime under 33ms — descriptor quality always trumps runtime, so the correct answer is always the most accurate detector once hardware constraints are removed

quizzes.question: You are building a dependency parser for a production NLP pipeline. You compare a transition-based parser (e.g., arc-eager) with a graph-based parser (e.g., Eisner's algorithm). Your dataset has sentences averaging 25 tokens, and you need to process 10,000 sentences per second at inference time. You also need to handle non-projective dependencies (common in free-word-order languages like Czech). Which statement BEST describes the tradeoff between these two approaches?

options.A: Transition-based parsers run in O(n) time and are faster at inference, but they cannot handle non-projective dependencies — making graph-based parsers necessary for free-word-order languages
options.B: Graph-based parsers always outperform transition-based parsers on accuracy because they search the full space of parse trees, so you should use graph-based despite the speed cost
options.C: Both parsers run in O(n²) time, so there is no speed difference — choose based solely on accuracy requirements
options.D: Transition-based parsers are faster (O(n)) and can handle non-projective dependencies if trained with a non-projective oracle, while graph-based parsers offer globally optimal solutions at O(n²) or O(n³) cost — the choice depends on whether global optimality justifies the latency budget

quizzes.question: You are implementing a feature matching pipeline for an augmented reality app that overlays virtual furniture onto a live camera feed of a room. The camera moves freely, causing perspective changes, and lighting varies as the user walks around. You compute SIFT descriptors for keypoints in a reference image and the live frame. To match descriptors, you apply Lowe's ratio test: for each descriptor in the reference image, you find its two nearest neighbors in the live frame and keep the match only if (distance to 1st nearest) / (distance to 2nd nearest) < threshold. What is the PRIMARY purpose of this ratio test?

options.A: It filters out keypoints that are not scale-invariant, ensuring only multi-scale features survive matching
options.B: It rejects ambiguous matches where the best match is not significantly better than the second-best, reducing false positives from repetitive textures or non-distinctive descriptors
options.C: It normalizes descriptor distances to account for illumination changes between the reference and live frames
options.D: It enforces geometric consistency by verifying that matched keypoints maintain their spatial arrangement across frames

quizzes.question: You are building a visual place recognition system for a self-driving car. The car stores a database of SIFT descriptors from reference images captured during daytime. At night, the car captures a new image and extracts SIFT descriptors. You match using brute-force L2 distance between 128-dimensional SIFT descriptor vectors. Despite SIFT's gradient histogram design, matching accuracy drops from 92% (day-to-day) to 58% (day-to-night). Which explanation BEST identifies WHY SIFT matching degrades under this illumination shift, and what mitigation would help?

options.A: SIFT descriptors are computed from raw pixel intensities, so any brightness change directly alters the descriptor values — switching to a binary descriptor like BRIEF would fix this since binary comparisons are illumination-invariant
options.B: SIFT's 128-D descriptor is based on gradient orientation histograms, which are largely invariant to linear illumination changes (brightness/contrast). However, day-to-night causes non-linear illumination shifts (e.g., artificial lighting, shadows, specular reflections) that alter local gradient distributions — using histogram equalization or training a learned descriptor (e.g., HardNet) on cross-illumination pairs would improve robustness
options.C: The degradation occurs because SIFT's Difference-of-Gaussian keypoint detector fails to fire at the same locations under different lighting, so no descriptors are extracted at corresponding points — increasing the contrast threshold would fix this
options.D: SIFT matching degrades because L2 distance is not a valid metric for high-dimensional vectors due to the curse of dimensionality — replacing L2 with cosine similarity would fully resolve the accuracy drop regardless of illumination changes

quizzes.question: You are training a 5-layer fully connected network for regression. During debugging, you compute the gradient of the loss with respect to the weights in layer 2 using backpropagation, and separately verify it using finite differences: ∂L/∂w ≈ [L(w+ε) − L(w−ε)] / (2ε). The relative difference between the two is 0.3 (30%). You then check layer 5 and find the relative difference is only 0.0001. Which of the following BEST explains this pattern?

options.A: The finite difference method is inaccurate for early layers because ε is too large — decreasing ε to 1e-10 would make both layers match equally well
options.B: There is likely a bug in the backpropagation implementation for the early layers — since backprop computes gradients via the chain rule, an error in any intermediate layer's local gradient will compound as it propagates backward, affecting earlier layers more than later ones
options.C: Layer 2 gradients are inherently less precise because they are smaller in magnitude due to vanishing gradients, making any numerical comparison unreliable
options.D: The discrepancy is expected and harmless — backpropagation only guarantees exact gradients for the last layer, and approximations grow for earlier layers as a fundamental limitation of the chain rule

quizzes.question: You are training an RL agent to navigate a 50×50 grid world with sparse rewards (reward of +100 only at the goal, 0 elsewhere). Using tabular Q-learning with ε-greedy exploration (ε=0.1), the agent fails to reach the goal within 10,000 episodes. You switch to the same Q-learning algorithm but replace ε-greedy with Boltzmann (softmax) exploration using a temperature parameter τ that anneals from 10.0 to 0.1 over training. The agent now consistently reaches the goal by episode 2,000. Which explanation BEST accounts for this improvement?

options.A: Boltzmann exploration converges faster because it always selects the highest Q-value action, eliminating wasted random steps that ε-greedy takes 10% of the time
options.B: High initial τ makes action selection nearly uniform (broad exploration), and as τ decreases, the agent increasingly exploits learned Q-values — this graduated shift from exploration to exploitation is more effective than ε-greedy's fixed 10% random rate, especially in sparse-reward environments where early broad exploration is critical to discovering the goal
options.C: Boltzmann exploration works better because it changes the Q-learning update rule to weight rewards by their probability, making the Bellman backup more accurate in sparse-reward settings
options.D: The improvement is due to the annealing schedule alone — if you annealed ε from 1.0 to 0.01 on the same schedule, ε-greedy would perform identically since both methods select random actions with the same frequency
